questions.question: Why does LLM-style audio generation need a neural codec at all?
options: To reduce file sizes for storage

Language models predict discrete tokens but audio is continuous — a learned encoder discretizes waveforms into a small token vocabulary, and a matching decoder reconstructs audio; MusicGen, Moshi, Sesame CSM, and Orpheus all build on this
Codecs add copyright protection
GPUs cannot process float audio
questions.explanation: The codec is the bridge that turned audio generation into next-token prediction — by 2026 almost all audio generation is tokens.

questions.question: How does Residual Vector Quantization (RVQ) achieve a huge effective vocabulary with small codebooks?
options: One giant codebook of a billion codes

A cascade: the first codebook quantizes the encoder output, the second quantizes the RESIDUAL, and so on — 8 codebooks of 1024 codes give an effective vocabulary of 1024⁸ ≈ 10²⁴; the decoder sums the chosen codes per frame
Hashing the waveform into 16 bits
Storing raw samples as integers
questions.explanation: A single codebook would need millions of codes for acceptable quality. The residual cascade is the core trick shared by EnCodec, DAC, SNAC, and Mimi.

questions.question: What is the semantic-acoustic split, and why does it matter?
options: Splitting stereo into two channels

Pure reconstruction codecs give blurry speech when generating from text because the LM must learn language AND acoustics in the same codebook — Mimi distills codebook 0 from WavLM to carry linguistic content, with codebooks 1-7 as acoustic residual; this split is what makes Moshi and Sesame CSM work
Separating music from speech files
Encoding loud and quiet parts separately
questions.explanation: The lesson calls it the most important audio architecture shift since the Transformer: semantic token = what was said; acoustic tokens = how it sounded (speaker, timbre, noise).

questions.question: Why do codec frame rates matter for language modeling?
options: Higher frame rates always sound better

Lower frame rate = shorter sequence = faster LM: at Mimi's 12.5 Hz a 10-second utterance is just 125 frames, versus 750+ at EnCodec's 75 Hz — easily within a transformer's comfortable prediction range
Frame rate only affects file extension
LMs require exactly 100 Hz inputs
questions.explanation: SNAC takes a hierarchical route: coarse codebooks at ~12 Hz sketch the audio while fine ones at 50 Hz add detail — a structure that maps well onto LM generation (used by Orpheus-3B).

questions.question: How do the four 2026 codecs divide the design space?
options: They are interchangeable forks of MP3

EnCodec (2022) is the general-purpose baseline used by MusicGen; DAC has the highest open reconstruction fidelity at 44.1 kHz full-band; SNAC is multi-scale/hierarchical for efficient AR generation; Mimi is the low-frame-rate semantic+acoustic codec for streaming speech
All four are closed commercial products
Only one supports stereo
questions.explanation: Choose by downstream job: music fidelity → DAC, LM efficiency → SNAC, streaming conversational speech → Mimi, general baseline → EnCodec.
